text: The surgeon places the camera port at the junction of the xiphoid process and sternum, typically over the eighth rib or seventh intercostal space, using a zero-degree camera for a vertical position. The medial twelve-millimeter port is positioned at the sixth intercostal space, as medial and low as possible, with approximately ten to twelve centimeters between the camera and medial ports. The twelve-millimeter system port is placed right on the diaphragm, intended for lobe extraction, with six to eight centimeters between the camera port and robotic arms for optimal spacing during the right lower lobectomy.

The surgeon pulls up on the lower lobe, applying tension to the inferior pulmonary vein, which is divided using bipolar cautery set to a high setting for minimal thermal spread and effective hemostasis. The inferior pulmonary ligament is divided, and station nine lymph nodes are dissected near the esophagus. The inferior pulmonary vein is exposed and encircled, with the phrenic nerve visible medially over the inferior vena cava. The lower lobe is reflected anteriorly, and the posterior hilum is divided, revealing station seven lymph nodes, which are dissected with caution due to their rich blood supply. The posterior aspect of the pericardium becomes visible, and sponge rolls are used to aid dissection of the posterior bronchus intermedius and right upper lobe bronchus, exposing the hilum from behind.

The surgeon reflects the lung caudad to dissect the superior hilum, exposing station two, four, and ten lymph nodes. The lower lobe is reflected inferiorly to expose the pulmonary artery within the fissure. The interlobar pulmonary artery is visible, along with the middle and upper lobes. The surgeon uses blunt dissection to expose the pulmonary artery at the confluence of the major and minor fissures. An endolead catheter and an angled tip stapler with a blue load are used to divide the posterior aspect of the major fissure. The surgeon identifies connective tissue and completes the division of the fissure, then pulls the lower lobe inferiorly to expose the medial side of the pulmonary artery and locate the middle lobe bronchus, which is protected before dividing the anterior aspect of the major fissure.

The surgeon uses an angle tip stapler and an endolead catheter to divide the medial aspect of the major fissure, ensuring the middle lobe bronchus is protected. The interlobar pulmonary artery is exposed, revealing the suprasegmental branch and basilar trunk. Blunt dissection is used to pass underneath the right lower lobe pulmonary artery, allowing both the suprasegmental and basilar branches to be divided together. The inferior pulmonary vein is encircled and divided with a white load stapler, completing the division of arterial blood supply and venous drainage, while ensuring the right middle lobe bronchus remains protected.

The surgeon carefully dissects the right lower lobe bronchus, separating it from the pulmonary artery to ensure a wide opening for the right middle lobe bronchus. The endolead catheter is placed underneath the bronchus, and an angle tip stapler with a green load is used to divide it. The right lower lobe is detached, and the lung is pushed towards the apex of the chest. The surgeon performs a superior hilar lymph node dissection, removing the lymph node packet with bipolar cautery, while protecting the phrenic nerve. Surgicel is packed for hemostasis, completing the mediastinal and hilar lymph node dissection.
